h1Title: Croup: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Croup — medically termed acute laryngotracheobronchitis — is a viral infection causing inflammation and subglottic edema of the larynx, trachea, and bronchi that produces the hallmark barking cough, stridor, and dysphonia in young children. Parainfluenza virus types 1 and 2 account for 75% of cases, with RSV, influenza, and adenovirus responsible for most of the remainder. It is the most common cause of acute upper airway obstruction in children, affecting approximately 3% of children aged 6 months to 3 years annually, with a peak incidence at 18-24 months. Symptoms typically begin at night with a sudden onset of barking cough and inspiratory stridor. A single oral or intramuscular dose of dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg) is the standard of care and reduces hospitalization by approximately 75%.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 3% of children aged 6 months to 3 years are affected annually in North America; croup accounts for 15% of all pediatric respiratory infections (Canadian Pediatric Society 2021)
keyStats.demographics: Boys affected 1.4 times more often than girls; peak incidence at 18-24 months; can occur from 3 months to 6 years but is uncommon after age 6; late autumn peak corresponds with parainfluenza type 1 epidemics
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence at 18-24 months; 60% of cases occur between ages 1 and 3 years; rare before 6 months and after 6 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 1.5-2 million croup episodes per year in the United States alone; emergency department visit rate approximately 5 per 1,000 children under 6 per year

definition: Croup (ICD-10: J05.0), formally acute laryngotracheobronchitis, is an acute inflammatory condition of the subglottic region of the larynx, trachea, and proximal bronchi caused predominantly by viral infection. The pathognomonic feature is subglottic narrowing visible on anteroposterior neck radiograph as the 'steeple sign' — symmetric subglottic narrowing at the level of the cricoid cartilage. Viral invasion of the respiratory epithelium triggers mucosal edema and secretion that critically narrows the subglottic airway; because the subglottis is the narrowest part of the pediatric airway and is bounded by the rigid cricoid ring, even 1 mm of circumferential edema reduces cross-sectional area by 44% and airway resistance by 75% (Poiseuille's law). The result is turbulent airflow manifesting as inspiratory stridor, a characteristic brassy barking cough, and dysphonia (hoarse voice). Croup is classified by severity: mild (occasional barking cough, mild stridor with agitation), moderate (stridor at rest, intercostal and sternal retractions, without agitation), and severe (stridor at rest, marked work of breathing, agitation, cyanosis, or altered consciousness). Pediatricians, emergency physicians, and pediatric pulmonologists manage this condition; most cases are managed in ambulatory settings.

typesClassification.description: The most common form, caused by parainfluenza virus types 1 and 2 (75% of cases). Begins as a 1-3 day coryzal prodrome followed by sudden onset of barking cough, stridor, and hoarse voice, typically at night. Resolves within 3-7 days in the majority of cases. Responds to a single dose of dexamethasone.

Recurrent non-febrile episodes of sudden nocturnal barking cough and stridor without viral prodrome, thought to be related to atopy and airway hyperresponsiveness rather than infection. Episodes are typically shorter (resolving within hours) and respond rapidly to fresh cool air or nebulized racemic epinephrine. Recurrent spasmodic croup warrants evaluation for subglottic stenosis or gastro-oesophageal reflux.

typesClassification.type: Croup due to influenza or RSV
typesClassification.description: More severe clinical course than parainfluenza croup. Influenza-associated croup has higher hospitalization rates; RSV-associated croup may be accompanied by lower respiratory tract involvement (bronchiolitis). Both require the same management as viral croup but with heightened monitoring.

Rare but life-threatening condition that mimics croup — Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) produces a pseudomembranous exudate in the trachea causing severe progressive stridor unresponsive to dexamethasone and epinephrine. Children appear toxic; require intubation, bronchoscopic debridement, and IV antibiotics.

primarySymptoms: Barking or seal-like cough — the pathognomonic feature, caused by subglottic edema and turbulent airflow through the narrowed subglottis; abrupt in onset and typically worsened by agitation or crying.

Inspiratory stridor — a high-pitched harsh sound produced by partial upper airway obstruction at the level of the subglottic larynx; initially heard only with agitation, progressing to stridor at rest in moderate-to-severe croup.

Hoarse voice (dysphonia) from laryngeal mucosal inflammation impairing vocal cord vibration; often accompanies the barking cough and is a useful differentiating feature from conditions that affect the trachea below the cords.
Coryzal prodrome — runny nose, mild sore throat, and low-grade fever for 1-3 days before barking cough onset, reflecting the underlying viral upper respiratory infection.
Nocturnal onset and worsening — symptoms characteristically worsen at night, possibly because of circadian changes in cortisol levels, drying of secretions, and increased vagal tone during sleep.
Respiratory distress signs in moderate-to-severe cases: intercostal, subcostal, or suprasternal retractions from increased work of breathing against the narrowed airway; nasal flaring; tachycardia.
Fever — present in approximately 50-60% of viral croup episodes, typically low-grade (37.5-38.5°C); high fever (above 39°C) persisting beyond 48 hours raises concern for bacterial superinfection.

Agitation and restlessness from air hunger in moderate-to-severe croup — a calm, pale, or lethargic child with stridor is more concerning than an agitated one, suggesting fatigue and impending respiratory failure.
Feeding difficulties — young infants and toddlers often feed less well during acute croup due to increased work of breathing.
Biphasic stridor (both inspiratory and expiratory) or progressive worsening despite treatment — suggests critical airway narrowing requiring emergency intervention.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden awakening from sleep with a barking, seal-like cough in a child aged 6 months to 3 years — classic croup onset warranting parental assessment and often a telephone consultation with a healthcare provider.
Progressive stridor that is now audible at rest rather than only with crying — transition from mild to moderate croup indicating need for medical evaluation and corticosteroid therapy.
Increasing retractions (visible inward movement of the chest wall between ribs on inspiration) — increasing work of breathing requiring prompt assessment.
Coryzal prodrome in a young child progressing to hoarse voice over 24-48 hours — predictive precursor to full croup syndrome.
Pallor or persistent agitation with stridulous breathing in a known croup episode — early signs of hypoxia preceding cyanosis.
emergencySigns: Cyanosis (bluish discoloration of lips or nail beds) with stridor — indicates critical hypoxia; requires immediate 100% oxygen and emergency airway intervention.
Fatigue, lethargy, or reduced consciousness in a child with stridor — exhausted respiratory muscles signal impending respiratory failure and require urgent intubation preparation.
Biphasic stridor (audible on both inspiration and expiration) at rest — indicates near-complete subglottic obstruction; emergent epinephrine and airway management required.

Drooling, toxic appearance, high fever, and inability to lie flat in a young child with stridor — suggests epiglottitis (H. influenzae type b in unvaccinated children) or bacterial tracheitis; do not examine the throat; maintain upright position and call for immediate airway support.
Failure to improve 30 minutes after nebulized epinephrine — requires hospital admission and preparation for potential intubation.

causes.description: The dominant cause of classic croup, responsible for 75% of cases. Parainfluenza type 1 produces biennial autumn epidemics in odd years; type 2 produces smaller outbreaks in even years. Type 3 causes year-round endemic croup. The virus replicates in the ciliated respiratory epithelium of the larynx and trachea, triggering mucosal edema that narrowing the subglottic airway.

Causes a variable proportion of croup (5-10%), particularly in children under 12 months. RSV croup tends to be more severe and more frequently associated with concurrent lower respiratory involvement (bronchiolitis). Autumn-winter seasonal pattern matches parainfluenza outbreaks in some regions.

Associated with more severe croup presentations and higher hospitalization rates than parainfluenza. Influenza-associated croup can occur in older children and adults in addition to the typical toddler age group. Annual influenza vaccination significantly reduces influenza-associated croup.

Causes 5-10% of croup cases and is associated with a more prolonged course and more prominent pharyngitis. Adenoviral croup tends to occur in older children and can present in older toddlers and school-age children.

Spasmodic croup — recurrent non-febrile episodes of sudden barking cough without viral prodrome — is associated with personal or family history of atopy, asthma, and eczema. Thought to involve IgE-mediated or hyperresponsive reaction to minor upper respiratory stimuli rather than a de novo viral infection. Gastro-oesophageal reflux may be a contributing trigger in infants with recurrent spasmodic croup.

riskFactors.description: The subglottic airway in this age group is both small in absolute diameter and bounded by the rigid cricoid ring, making it uniquely vulnerable to clinically significant obstruction from mucosal edema. Peak incidence at 18-24 months. The incidence of croup falls sharply after age 3 as the airway matures.

Boys are affected 1.4 times more frequently than girls in most epidemiological studies. The mechanism is uncertain but may relate to slightly smaller male subglottic diameters relative to body size in early childhood.

Parainfluenza type 1 causes biennial autumn epidemics in odd-numbered years in temperate climates. Healthcare systems see pronounced croup surges every two years. RSV co-epidemics in autumn-winter further increase the burden.

Children with atopy have significantly higher rates of recurrent croup (spasmodic croup) and more severe responses to upper respiratory infections. Atopy may predispose through enhanced mucosal inflammatory responses and airway hyperresponsiveness.

Passive smoke exposure impairs mucociliary clearance, increases airway hyperresponsiveness, and is associated with more frequent and more severe croup episodes. Eliminating household smoking measurably reduces recurrent upper respiratory illness in young children.

Children who have had croup once are significantly more likely to have recurrent episodes. Some children appear to have constitutionally reactive subglottic airways; recurrent spasmodic croup warrants evaluation for subglottic stenosis, laryngomalacia, or haemangioma in children under 6 months.

affectedDemographics: Children aged 6 months to 3 years bear the vast majority of the burden; 60% of all croup cases occur between ages 1 and 3 years (CDC and Canadian Pediatric Society data).
Boys have 1.4 times the incidence of girls; croup is one of the few respiratory infections with a consistently male preponderance in young children.
Children with asthma, atopy, or a family history of atopy have substantially higher rates of recurrent spasmodic croup than children without atopic conditions.

Unvaccinated children are at higher risk of epiglottitis from H. influenzae type b — a croup mimic with far higher mortality; Hib vaccination has reduced epiglottitis by over 90% in vaccinated populations.
diagnosisOverview: Croup is a clinical diagnosis in the vast majority of cases; no investigations are needed when the presentation is typical. The characteristic triad of barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and hoarse voice in a child aged 6 months to 3 years following a coryzal prodrome is diagnostic. Severity is assessed using the Westley Croup Score, which assigns points for level of consciousness, cyanosis, stridor, air entry, and retractions, categorizing severity as mild (score 0-2), moderate (3-7), or severe (8+). Oxygen saturation by pulse oximetry is the only investigation routinely needed; hypoxia (SpO2 below 92%) warrants supplemental oxygen and escalated management. Anteroposterior neck X-ray showing the steeple sign (subglottic narrowing) supports the diagnosis but is not required and can precipitate agitation and airway compromise if the child is distressed. Plain neck radiography has poor sensitivity and is reserved for atypical presentations where epiglottitis or foreign body is in the differential. Lateral neck radiograph showing an enlarged epiglottis (thumbprint sign) or distended hypopharynx suggests epiglottitis — do not examine the throat; arrange immediate controlled airway management. Bacterial tracheitis presents with high fever, toxicity, failure to respond to standard croup therapy, and irregular tracheal narrowing on radiograph rather than smooth steeple tapering. Blood tests are not routinely indicated but may be needed for suspected bacterial tracheitis (leukocytosis, elevated CRP).

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical assessment and Westley Croup Score
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses croup and stratifies severity to guide treatment intensity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician observes the child's breathing and listens to the chest; scoring takes 1-2 minutes and guides whether home management, emergency department observation, or hospital admission is appropriate. No painful procedures are needed for mild-moderate croup — minimizing distress is critical because agitation worsens stridor.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects hypoxia indicating critical airway narrowing; guides oxygen supplementation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clip on the fingertip or toe, painless and takes 30 seconds to 1 minute. Oxygen saturation below 92% on room air is a red flag requiring escalated management. Normal oxygen saturation does not exclude significant croup but provides reassurance.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports diagnosis when presentation is atypical; shows subglottic narrowing in croup
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A plain radiograph of the neck in the AP projection takes about 2 minutes. The child should be kept calm and ideally remain with parents. Not indicated routinely; reserved for atypical presentations. The steeple sign is present in about 50% of croup cases and not specific.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Shows epiglottis size and hypopharyngeal configuration when epiglottitis cannot be excluded clinically
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in stable, upright children only. If epiglottitis is suspected, imaging should not delay airway management. The thumbprint sign (enlarged epiglottis) and distended hypopharynx differentiate epiglottitis from croup.

diagnosticTests.test: Nasopharyngeal viral swab for respiratory PCR panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the specific viral pathogen; useful in hospitalized patients, immunocompromised children, or outbreak investigation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A brief nasopharyngeal swab by a healthcare worker. Mild discomfort. Results in 4-8 hours. Not needed for routine outpatient croup but guides cohorting in hospital and informs public health data.

treatmentOverview: The cornerstone of croup management is corticosteroid therapy, which rapidly reduces subglottic mucosal edema by suppressing the inflammatory cascade. A single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg (maximum 10 mg) is the evidence-based standard of care, with effect within 2-4 hours, reducing croup symptoms, emergency department return visits, and hospitalization by approximately 75% in multiple RCTs. Oral and intramuscular dexamethasone are equally effective; oral administration is preferred in cooperative children. Budesonide 2 mg nebulized is an alternative when oral administration is not possible. For moderate-to-severe croup with stridor at rest, nebulized racemic epinephrine (L-epinephrine 5 mL of 0.1% or racemic epinephrine 0.5 mL of 2.25% in 2.5 mL saline) reduces airway edema within minutes through alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction of subglottic vessels. The epinephrine effect is transient (60-90 minutes); children who receive nebulized epinephrine must be observed for a minimum of 3 hours for rebound worsening before discharge. Heliox (70-30% helium-oxygen mixture) reduces turbulent airflow in severe croup and may temporize while other therapies take effect; evidence is limited. Supplemental oxygen is given for SpO2 below 92%, using the least distressing delivery method (blow-by technique for young children if mask is not tolerated). Humidified air was historically recommended but multiple RCTs including Johnson 1996 and Scolnik 2006 failed to show benefit over dry air. Antibiotics are not indicated for viral croup. Bacterial tracheitis requires urgent intubation, bronchoscopic debridement, and anti-staphylococcal IV antibiotics. All children with moderate-to-severe croup or those requiring epinephrine should be admitted for observation.

medicalTreatments.name: Dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg orally or IM, maximum 10 mg, single dose)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for all but the mildest croup per Pediatric Emergency Research Network guidelines. Synthetic glucocorticoid with 25× potency of hydrocortisone; reduces subglottic mucosal edema by suppressing cytokine-mediated inflammation. Effect begins within 2-4 hours and lasts 24-48 hours. Single dose; no repeat dosing needed in typical cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces duration of symptoms by approximately 12 hours vs placebo; reduces return emergency department visits by 50-75%; reduces hospitalization rate from 15% to under 5% in meta-analyses

medicalTreatments.name: Nebulized racemic epinephrine (L-epinephrine 5 mL of 0.1%, or racemic epinephrine 0.5 mL of 2.25%)
medicalTreatments.description: Alpha-adrenergic agonist that causes submucosal vasoconstriction and rapid reduction in mucosal edema. Indicated for moderate-to-severe croup with stridor at rest. Effect begins within 10-30 minutes. Duration 60-90 minutes; children must be observed for at least 3 hours after administration for rebound.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Dramatic rapid symptom reduction in severe croup; reduces need for intubation from approximately 1-2% to under 0.3% in treated patients; Westley score improvement of 3+ points within 30 minutes in 80-90% of cases

medicalTreatments.name: Nebulized budesonide (2 mg in 4 mL saline, single dose)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative corticosteroid for croup when oral administration is not possible — in a vomiting child, uncooperative child, or after failed oral dexamethasone. Equivalent efficacy to oral dexamethasone in head-to-head RCTs. Onset similar to oral route.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent to oral dexamethasone in RCTs; reduces symptoms and hospitalization at similar rates

medicalTreatments.name: Supplemental oxygen (blow-by technique or face mask, target SpO2 ≥92%)
medicalTreatments.description: Provided for hypoxia (SpO2 below 92%) or severe respiratory distress. Blow-by technique (flowing oxygen held near the child's face without direct mask contact) is preferred in distressed toddlers to minimize agitation, which worsens stridor. Do not force an oxygen mask onto a severely stridulous child — airway obstruction from distress is dangerous.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects hypoxia and reduces work of breathing; prioritized when saturation is below 92%; no role in mild-moderate croup with normal saturations

medicalTreatments.name: Heliox (70:30 helium-oxygen mixture)
medicalTreatments.description: Low-density gas mixture that reduces turbulent-to-laminar airflow conversion in the narrowed subglottis, reducing work of breathing. Reserved for severe croup with impending respiratory failure as a bridge to definitive management. Not first-line and not widely available in all emergency departments.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Small RCTs show reduced Westley score at 60 minutes vs standard oxygen; limited by availability and logistical complexity

surgicalOptions.name: Endotracheal intubation for respiratory failure
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for the 1-2% of hospitalized croup patients who progress despite maximal medical therapy — nebulized epinephrine, dexamethasone, and heliox. Nasotracheal intubation with a tube 0.5-1 mm smaller than calculated diameter is performed in a controlled setting (operating room or pediatric intensive care with direct laryngoscopy or video laryngoscopy). Extubation is typically possible within 24-48 hours as edema resolves.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Extubation within 24-48 hours in over 95% of intubated croup patients; mortality from intubated viral croup in modern ICUs is under 0.5%

surgicalOptions.name: Bronchoscopic debridement for bacterial tracheitis
surgicalOptions.description: Rigid or flexible bronchoscopy under general anesthesia to remove pseudomembranous tracheal exudate in bacterial tracheitis. Combined with endotracheal intubation and prolonged IV anti-staphylococcal antibiotic therapy. Required in virtually all cases of confirmed bacterial tracheitis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful airway clearance in the majority of cases; hospital stay of 7-14 days with appropriate antibiotics and intensive care; mortality under 5% in modern series

alternativeTreatments.name: Cool night air or cool-mist exposure
alternativeTreatments.description: Parents anecdotally report improvement after taking a child outdoors into cool night air or sitting in a bathroom with cold water running. Physiological plausibility: cool dry air may reduce mucosal edema. Formal RCTs show no significant benefit from cool mist over room air, but the intervention is harmless and may relieve mild spasmodic croup through reflex mechanisms.

whySeeSpecialist: Most croup is managed in primary care or emergency departments without specialist involvement. Pediatric pulmonology or ENT referral is appropriate for recurrent croup (more than two episodes per year), croup in infants under 6 months (where structural causes such as subglottic stenosis, laryngomalacia, or haemangioma must be excluded), failure to respond to standard dexamethasone and epinephrine, or for direct laryngoscopy when bacterial tracheitis or fixed subglottic obstruction is suspected.
doctorSelectionGuide: For an acute episode, go to the nearest emergency department or call the pediatric emergency helpline. Most acute croup can be managed by a pediatric emergency physician or experienced general practitioner. For recurrent episodes, request a pediatric ENT or pediatric pulmonologist who performs flexible or rigid laryngoscopy to assess the airway anatomy and exclude structural causes.
hospitalCriteria: Moderate-to-severe croup with stridor at rest and retractions, or Westley score above 3
Any child who receives nebulized epinephrine — must be observed minimum 3 hours; admit if symptoms recur or persist
Oxygen saturation below 92% on room air requiring supplemental oxygen
Failure to improve with oral dexamethasone and nebulized budesonide after 4 hours of observation
Age under 6 months with croup (higher risk of structural airway anomaly and rapid decompensation)
Social concerns precluding safe home monitoring
keyFacilities: Pediatric emergency departments with nebulizer capability and pulse oximetry
Pediatric intensive care units for severe croup requiring intubation
Pediatric ENT departments for laryngoscopy in recurrent or atypical croup
Primary care clinics and pediatric after-hours services for mild croup assessment and dexamethasone dispensing
preventionStrategies: Annual influenza vaccination for all children 6 months and older reduces influenza-associated croup by preventing the primary viral trigger.
RSV immunization with nirsevimab (Beyfortus) for infants under 8 months during their first RSV season reduces severe RSV-associated respiratory disease including RSV croup.
Ensure all children receive Hib (Haemophilus influenzae type b) vaccination, which has reduced epiglottitis — a life-threatening croup mimic — by over 90% in vaccinated populations.
Minimize household cigarette smoke exposure — passive smoking increases the frequency and severity of recurrent upper respiratory infections and croup in young children.
Frequent hand washing and surface cleaning during autumn respiratory virus seasons reduces parainfluenza and RSV transmission within households and day care settings.

For children with known recurrent spasmodic croup: work with a pediatrician to identify and treat atopic triggers, gastro-oesophageal reflux, and to keep dexamethasone syrup prescribed at home for immediate use at onset.
lifestyleModifications: Keep the child calm during a croup episode — agitation and crying worsen stridor by increasing turbulent airflow; hold and comfort the child in an upright position, preferably in a parent's lap.
Minimize unnecessary interventions that agitate the child during moderate-to-severe croup, including physical examination of the throat with a tongue depressor.
Keep prescribed dexamethasone syrup (0.6 mg/kg dose) at home for children with recurrent croup, pre-prepared with parent instructions on when and how to give it.
Open a window or take the child outside into cool night air during mild spasmodic croup — anecdotal relief is common and the intervention is harmless.
Maintain hydration with small frequent sips of cool water or oral rehydration fluid during croup, especially if fever is present.
Contact the GP or emergency department early rather than waiting for severe distress — earlier dexamethasone treatment produces better outcomes.

recommended: Cool fluids (water, apple juice, oral rehydration solution) to maintain hydration and provide mild soothing of the inflamed upper airway
Small frequent sips rather than large volumes, as swallowing effort can transiently worsen stridor in moderate croup
Age-appropriate soft foods when appetite returns; avoid hard or crunchy foods that require effortful swallowing while the child is unwell
avoid: Forcing feeds during an acute episode — the child's energy should not be diverted to eating when respiratory work is increased
Hot foods or beverages during the acute phase — avoid adding thermal irritation to an already inflamed subglottic airway
Dairy products in infants who appear to have more secretions, if parents observe a consistent association (evidence is weak but the observation is harmless to act on)

exerciseGuidelines: Rest during acute croup. The child should be kept calm and still; physical activity and play that causes crying or exertion worsen stridor through increased turbulent airflow demands. Once symptoms have fully resolved (usually within 3-7 days), normal activity can resume. There are no specific exercise restrictions after recovery.
dailyManagement: Monitor respiratory rate and work of breathing: count breaths per minute for one full minute — a rate above 40 in a toddler at rest warrants medical attention.
Observe for retractions (inward movement of the chest wall between the ribs, above the sternum, or below the rib cage) — new or worsening retractions indicate increasing airway obstruction.
Administer oral dexamethasone at the first sign of moderate croup (stridor at rest or visible retractions) rather than waiting to see if symptoms progress.
Keep the child upright — reclined or supine positioning worsens stridor due to gravity-dependent airway narrowing.

Call emergency services or take to the emergency department immediately if cyanosis appears, the child becomes unusually still and pale, stridor becomes biphasic, or the child stops vocalizing entirely.
After the acute episode: document the episode for follow-up with the pediatrician; children with recurrent croup need laryngoscopic assessment to exclude structural causes.
prognosis: The prognosis for viral croup in otherwise healthy children is excellent. Over 85% of cases are mild and can be managed at home with oral dexamethasone. Hospitalization is required in approximately 5-8% of emergency department visits, primarily for moderate-to-severe disease requiring nebulized epinephrine and monitoring. Endotracheal intubation is needed in fewer than 1-2% of hospitalized croup patients. Fatalities from viral croup are extremely rare in developed settings with access to corticosteroid therapy and emergency airway management. Bacterial tracheitis carries higher morbidity, requiring ICU admission and intubation in the majority of cases, but modern mortality is below 5% with appropriate management. Recurrent spasmodic croup typically resolves without specific intervention as the child's airway matures, usually by age 6-8 years. Children with structural airway anomalies (subglottic stenosis, haemangioma) require laryngoscopic diagnosis and specific intervention. The frequency of croup episodes declines substantially after age 3 as the subglottic airway matures and cross-reactive viral immunity accumulates.
recoveryTimeline: Most acute viral croup peaks over 24-48 hours and fully resolves within 3-7 days. Barking cough typically resolves within 48-72 hours of dexamethasone treatment. Residual mild hoarseness or cough can persist for up to 2 weeks as laryngeal mucosal inflammation subsides. Children who receive emergency epinephrine should be observed for 3-4 hours before discharge.
complications: Respiratory failure requiring intubation — occurs in fewer than 1-2% of hospitalized croup patients; managed in pediatric ICU with nasotracheal intubation and extubation within 24-48 hours as edema resolves.

Bacterial tracheitis secondary to viral croup — S. aureus superinfection of the trachea produces a pseudomembranous exudate causing life-threatening airway obstruction; presents as croup unresponsive to standard treatment with toxic appearance and high fever.

Pulmonary edema — rare complication of severe upper airway obstruction (negative-pressure pulmonary edema) after forceful inspiratory efforts against a closed airway; resolves with positive pressure ventilation.

Misdiagnosis of epiglottitis as croup — epiglottitis is immediately life-threatening; failure to recognize it delays controlled airway management. Key distinguishing features: toxic appearance, drooling, preference for tripod position, minimal barking cough.

Recurrent croup with structural airway pathology — children under 6 months with croup or those with more than two episodes per year should be evaluated with laryngoscopy to exclude subglottic stenosis, haemangioma, or papillomatosis.

supportResources.name: Canadian Paediatric Society — Croup
supportResources.description: Parent-facing guidance from the Canadian Paediatric Society on recognizing croup severity, home management, and when to seek emergency care.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Pediatrics — HealthyChildren.org: Croup
supportResources.description: AAP patient information on croup causes, treatment at home and in hospital, and prevention strategies for parents.

supportResources.name: Lucile Packard Children's Hospital — Croup Management Guide
supportResources.description: Detailed patient and family guide from a leading US pediatric center on croup stages, treatments, and emergency symptoms.

confusedWithConditions.name: Epiglottitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Epiglottitis (inflammation of the epiglottis, typically from H. influenzae type b or S. aureus) presents with abrupt severe stridor, drooling, toxic appearance, high fever, and a preference for sitting upright in a tripod position — without the barking cough of croup. Children with epiglottitis appear very sick and do not vocalize much. Throat examination must not be attempted. Lateral neck radiograph shows the thumbprint sign; croup shows the steeple sign on AP.

confusedWithConditions.name: Common Cold
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The common cold causes nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, and mild cough without stridor, hoarseness, or significant respiratory distress. Croup produces the distinctive barking cough and inspiratory stridor from subglottic narrowing. Stridor is never a feature of the common cold and always warrants clinical evaluation in a young child.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Bronchiolitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bronchiolitis predominantly affects children under 12 months and causes lower airway disease — wheeze, tachypnoea, and widespread fine crackles from small airway inflammation. Croup affects the upper airway and causes stridor and barking cough. Bronchiolitis affects smaller airways throughout the lung; croup narrows the subglottis. Both may be caused by RSV.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Tonsillitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tonsillitis produces pharyngeal inflammation with exudate, sore throat, and adenopathy but does not cause stridor or barking cough. The cough of tonsillitis is a moist, wet cough from postnasal drip; the croup cough is brassy and barking. Tonsillar enlargement can occasionally cause stridor but is visible on examination.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Bronchiolitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are common viral lower and upper respiratory infections in young children caused by RSV and parainfluenza; bronchiolitis affects the lower airway, croup the upper airway; RSV can cause both simultaneously in infants.

relatedConditions.name: Common Cold
relatedConditions.relevance: Croup follows a 1-3 day coryzal prodrome indistinguishable from a common cold; parainfluenza virus causes both conditions, with croup occurring when viral tropism extends to the subglottic larynx.

relatedConditions.name: Epiglottitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most important life-threatening differential diagnosis of croup; epiglottitis requires immediate airway management while croup is managed medically; distinguishing them is a critical clinical skill.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Upper Respiratory Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Croup is a specific form of upper respiratory infection defined by subglottic involvement; it occurs in the context of broader upper respiratory viral infection.

faqs.question: What does croup sound like?
faqs.answer: Croup causes a distinctive barking or seal-like cough that is often described as resembling a barking dog or a seal. The sound comes from air passing through a swollen, narrowed subglottic (below the vocal cord) airway. A harsh, high-pitched breathing sound on inhalation — called stridor — may also be present in moderate croup.

faqs.question: What age does croup affect?
faqs.answer: Croup most commonly affects children between 6 months and 3 years of age, with a peak at 18-24 months. It can occur in children up to age 6 but becomes less common as the airway grows and immunity to parainfluenza virus develops. Croup is rare in children over 6 and in adults.

faqs.question: How do you treat croup at home?
faqs.answer: Mild croup can be managed at home with oral dexamethasone (given or prescribed by a doctor), keeping the child calm, using cool night air or cool mist, and ensuring adequate fluid intake. Ibuprofen or paracetamol can be given for fever and discomfort. Seek immediate care if stridor is present at rest, breathing becomes labored, or the child turns pale or blue.

faqs.question: Does dexamethasone cure croup?
faqs.answer: A single dose of dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg) does not cure croup but dramatically reduces severity, shortens duration, and prevents hospitalization. It suppresses the subglottic mucosal inflammation caused by the virus. The viral infection still runs its course, but symptoms are substantially milder and the risk of progression to severe obstruction is much lower.

faqs.question: How long does croup last?
faqs.answer: Most croup episodes last 3-7 days. Barking cough is usually worst on nights one and two and significantly better by day three. Mild hoarseness and residual cough can persist for up to two weeks. Spasmodic croup episodes are shorter, often resolving within hours.

faqs.question: Is croup dangerous?
faqs.answer: Mild croup (the majority of cases) is not dangerous and resolves with outpatient treatment. Moderate-to-severe croup requiring emergency care occurs in about 5-8% of cases. Croup becomes dangerous if the airway swells enough to prevent adequate breathing — signs include stridor at rest, retractions, pallor, or blue discoloration. Deaths from viral croup are extremely rare in settings with access to corticosteroids.

faqs.question: Is croup contagious?
faqs.answer: Yes. Croup is caused by viruses (predominantly parainfluenza) that spread via respiratory droplets and surface contact. The virus itself is highly contagious, but not every infected contact will develop croup — most adults and older children get a mild cold from the same virus. Good hand hygiene reduces transmission.

faqs.question: Why does croup get worse at night?
faqs.answer: Croup symptoms typically worsen at night for several reasons: circadian dips in cortisol levels reduce natural anti-inflammatory protection, horizontal positioning increases upper airway edema, dry indoor air desiccates secretions, and reduced airway tone during sleep increases susceptibility to turbulent airflow. Most episodes resolve by morning.

faqs.question: What is the difference between croup and epiglottitis?
faqs.answer: Croup causes a barking cough, mild-to-moderate stridor, and a mildly unwell child who can vocalize and lie down. Epiglottitis causes a very sick, high-fever, drooling child who prefers to sit forward in a tripod position with no barking cough. Epiglottitis is an airway emergency. Never examine the throat if epiglottitis is suspected.

faqs.question: Does nebulized steam help croup?
faqs.answer: Steam and warm humidified air have been studied in multiple RCTs for croup and failed to show benefit over dry air. Cool mist also shows no statistically significant benefit in RCTs. Despite this, many parents report improvement with cool air. The mechanism may involve a mild reflex response rather than direct reduction of edema. These interventions are harmless and acceptable.

faqs.question: Can babies get croup?
faqs.answer: Croup can occur in infants as young as 3 months, though it is uncommon under 6 months. Any infant under 6 months with stridor requires urgent medical evaluation because structural airway problems (laryngomalacia, subglottic haemangioma, subglottic stenosis) are much more common at this age and need to be distinguished from viral croup.

faqs.question: Can croup recur?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recurrent croup occurs in 5-10% of affected children. Children who have had croup once are at higher risk of subsequent episodes with future viral infections. More than two episodes per year, or any episode in an infant under 6 months, warrants specialist laryngoscopy to exclude structural airway abnormalities.

faqs.question: When should I take my child to hospital for croup?
faqs.answer: Go to the emergency department if your child has stridor at rest (not just when crying), visible chest retractions, oxygen saturation below 92%, turning pale or blue, becoming unusually still or drowsy, drooling, or worsening symptoms after initial improvement. A child who has received nebulized epinephrine must be observed in hospital for at least 3 hours.

faqs.question: What causes croup?
faqs.answer: Parainfluenza virus types 1 and 2 cause approximately 75% of croup cases; RSV, influenza A and B, and adenovirus account for most of the remainder. The virus infects the subglottic larynx and trachea, causing mucosal edema that narrows the airway and produces the characteristic barking cough and stridor.

faqs.question: Can croup be prevented with a vaccine?
faqs.answer: Influenza vaccination reduces influenza-associated croup. RSV vaccination with nirsevimab (Beyfortus) now protects infants from severe RSV disease. Hib vaccination prevents epiglottitis, the most dangerous croup mimic. There is no specific parainfluenza vaccine currently in routine use, though candidates are in clinical trials.
